details.text: Mohism nearly rivaled Confucianism in influence during the Warring States period but largely disappeared after the Qin unification. Modern scholars have noted Mohist logic, optics experiments, and siege engineering as early contributions to scientific thought.

events.label: Early Wall Construction
events.description: Northern states built walls against nomadic raids — the precursors to the Great Wall. Qin, Zhao, and Yan all constructed long defensive barriers along their borders.

details.label: Background
details.text: The Warring States' northern borders faced constant pressure from nomadic peoples — Xiongnu, Donghu, Rong, and Di — who raided agricultural settlements for grain and livestock. Each northern state needed a defensive solution it could maintain independently.

details.label: The Structure
details.text: Qin, Zhao, and Yan each built rammed-earth walls along their northern frontiers, with watchtowers at regular intervals to signal approaching raiders. These walls were not connected — each state maintained its own section against its own nomadic neighbors.

details.label: Legacy
details.text: When the First Emperor of Qin unified China in 221 BCE, he connected and extended these walls into the first continuous Great Wall — conscripting hundreds of thousands to build what became one of history's most recognizable structures.

events.label: Jixia Academy
events.description: The Academy of Jixia in Qi was the world's first state-sponsored research institution, hosting scholars from every philosophical school for open debate.

details.label: Background
details.text: The rulers of Qi, seeking to attract the best minds to strengthen their state, established a permanent academy near their capital Linzi. Scholars received stipends, housing, and honors simply to debate and write — no immediate practical output required.

details.label: What Happened
details.text: Jixia hosted hundreds of scholars simultaneously from Confucian, Daoist, Legalist, Mohist, and Naturalist schools. Mencius, Xunzi, and many other major philosophers spent time there. The open debate format produced cross-fertilization between traditions.

details.label: Why It Matters
details.text: Jixia was the world's first state-funded research institution — a model that prefigured the university system. It demonstrated that intellectual diversity across competing schools, not orthodoxy, produced civilization's best thinking.

events.label: Linzi — Warring States Metropolis
events.description: Linzi, capital of Qi, grew into a city of 300,000–700,000 people — the largest city on Earth during the Warring States period — a center of trade, philosophy, and iron manufacturing.

details.label: Background
details.text: Qi's wealth from salt, iron, and fishing created a massive commercial city. By the 4th century BCE, Linzi housed the Jixia Academy, state arsenals, vast iron foundries, and a merchant population that made it the dominant economic center of the Zhou world.

details.label: The Structure
details.text: Ancient sources describe streets so crowded that carriages scraped wheels and pedestrians jostled shoulders. Archaeological excavations have confirmed city walls enclosing 15 sq km with densely packed residential and industrial zones — evidence of a true urban metropolis.

details.label: Legacy
details.text: Linzi's scale demonstrates that Warring States China had developed urbanization and commercial economies far beyond what Western historians once assumed. Its destruction by Qin in 221 BCE ended the most cosmopolitan city of the ancient world outside the Mediterranean.

events.label: Mencius
events.description: Mencius developed Confucian thought by arguing that human nature is inherently good and that rulers who fail their people deserve to be overthrown — a democratic seed in Confucian soil.

details.label: Who They Were
details.text: Meng Ke (Mencius) was raised by a famously devoted mother who moved three times to ensure he grew up near educational influences. He became the most important Confucian thinker after Confucius himself, synthesizing and extending Confucian ethics.

details.label: Role in the Zhou Era
details.text: Mencius argued that human nature is inherently good (ren xing shan) — containing moral seeds of benevolence, righteousness, propriety, and wisdom. He also argued that the people are the most important element in a kingdom; a ruler who fails them loses the Mandate of Heaven and may be overthrown.

details.label: Legacy
details.text: Mencius's arguments for human goodness and proto-democratic accountability became core Confucian doctrine. His book, the Mencius, became the fourth of the Four Books that formed the standard curriculum of Chinese education from the Song dynasty to the 20th century.

events.label: Zhuangzi
events.description: Zhuangzi expanded Daoist philosophy with paradoxical stories and humor, including the famous butterfly dream questioning the nature of reality — 'Am I a man dreaming I'm a butterfly?'

details.label: Who They Were
details.text: Zhuang Zhou was a low-ranking official who rejected offers of high position to maintain his intellectual freedom. Unlike Confucians who traveled between courts seeking employment, he preferred poverty and independence, reportedly comparing a chief minister's post to a sacrificial ox.

details.label: Role in the Zhou Era
details.text: Zhuangzi developed Daoism into a rich literature of parables and thought experiments. His butterfly dream, the Cook Ding story (perfect skill as union with the Dao), and debates with death explore the relativity of perspective and the limitations of reason.

details.label: Legacy
details.text: The Zhuangzi book is one of the greatest works of world literature — funny, profound, and philosophically sophisticated. Its influence on Chinese Buddhism, art, and poetry was immense. The story of Cook Ding remains one of the most discussed texts in Chinese philosophy.

events.label: Shang Yang
events.description: Shang Yang transformed Qin into a totalitarian military state through Legalist reforms: abolishing aristocratic privilege, rewarding military merit, and punishing dissent.

details.label: Who They Were
details.text: Shang Yang (Wei Yang) was a minister from the state of Wei who found patronage in Qin after failing to gain influence at Wei's court. He convinced Duke Xiao of Qin to implement radical Legalist restructuring of the entire state — a transformation so complete it shocked the Zhou world.

details.label: Role in the Zhou Era
details.text: His reforms abolished hereditary aristocracy, replacing it with a 20-rank system based purely on battlefield merit (measured in enemy heads). He organized the population into mutual-surveillance groups of five families, standardized weights and measures, and concentrated all power in the ruler.

details.label: Legacy
details.text: Shang Yang was ultimately executed by the same harsh laws he had created — torn apart by chariots after Duke Xiao died. But his reforms endured, transforming Qin into the most efficient military state in China and enabling the unification of 221 BCE.

events.label: Qu Yuan
events.description: The poet Qu Yuan, exiled from the state of Chu, drowned himself in the Miluo River in despair. The Dragon Boat Festival commemorates the search for his body.

details.label: Who They Were
details.text: Qu Yuan was a high minister of the state of Chu who was exiled by a jealous rival after falling out of royal favor. During his years of wandering, he composed some of Chinese literature's greatest poems, including the Li Sao ('Encountering Sorrow').

details.label: Role in the Zhou Era
details.text: Qu Yuan's poetry, collected in the Chu Ci anthology, introduced lyric poetry and shamanistic imagery alien to the Shijing's folk tradition. His despair at Chu's political corruption and eventual conquest by Qin is the emotional center of his work.

details.label: Legacy
details.text: When Qin finally conquered Chu, Qu Yuan drowned himself in the Miluo River. The Dragon Boat Festival — rice dumplings thrown into water to feed fish, boats searching for his body — is one of China's most beloved holidays, still celebrated by hundreds of millions annually.

events.label: Xunzi
events.description: Xunzi argued that human nature is inherently selfish and requires education and ritual to become good — the opposite of Mencius. His students Han Feizi and Li Si built the Qin Empire.

details.label: Who They Were
details.text: Xun Kuang (Xunzi) was a Confucian scholar who taught at the Jixia Academy and served as a magistrate. His position — that human nature is evil (xing e) and requires ritual and education to become good — was the polar opposite of Mencius and sparked controversy within Confucianism.

details.label: Role in the Zhou Era
details.text: Xunzi synthesized Confucian ritual with a hard-headed realism about human nature. His two most famous students, Han Feizi and Li Si, took his insights about human self-interest and built them into Legalist statecraft — the ideology that built the Qin Empire.

details.label: Legacy
details.text: Xunzi represents the hinge between Zhou Confucianism and Qin Legalism. His influence on Han Feizi and Li Si made him indirectly responsible for the unification of China — a Confucian whose students built an anti-Confucian empire.

events.label: Han Feizi and Legalism
events.description: Han Feizi synthesized Legalist thought into a systematic theory of authoritarian statecraft: clear laws, harsh punishments, and techniques of power. His ideas built the Qin state.

details.label: Context
details.text: Han Feizi was a prince of the state of Han who stuttered and could not speak well but wrote brilliantly. His synthesis of Legalist thought — drawing on Shen Buhai's techniques of power, Shen Dao's situational authority, and Shang Yang's law — created a comprehensive theory of autocratic governance.

details.label: The Work
details.text: The Han Feizi text argues that a ruler must use three tools: law (fa), technique (shu), and power/position (shi). Rulers should never show favoritism, should reward only measurable merit, and should maintain mystery and unpredictability to prevent manipulation by ministers.

details.label: Impact
details.text: When the king of Qin (future First Emperor) read Han Feizi's essays, he reportedly said 'If I could meet this man, I would die without regret.' He summoned Han Feizi to court — then had him imprisoned and forced to suicide, possibly fearing his influence. His ideas built the Qin Empire anyway.

events.label: Battle of Changping
events.description: The state of Qin annihilated 400,000 Zhao soldiers at Changping — one of the bloodiest battles in ancient history — effectively deciding the Warring States contest.

details.label: Background
details.text: Zhao and Qin had been competing for control of the upper Yellow River valley. When Zhao replaced its experienced defensive general Lian Po with the younger, more aggressive Zhao Kuo (who knew tactics only from books), Qin's general Bai Qi saw his opportunity.

details.label: The Battle
details.text: Bai Qi lured Zhao Kuo's army into an advance, then used flanking cavalry to cut off their supply lines. The Zhao army was surrounded for 46 days, then surrendered. Bai Qi reportedly executed the 400,000 prisoners — only 240 young soldiers were released.

details.label: Casualties & Aftermath
details.text: Changping removed Zhao as a viable counterweight to Qin. Ancient sources' figure of 400,000 dead may be exaggerated, but the battle clearly annihilated the Zhao military. It is considered the decisive moment when Qin's eventual unification of China became inevitable.

events.label: Dujiangyan Irrigation System
events.description: Li Bing built the Dujiangyan irrigation system in Sichuan, a masterwork of hydraulic engineering that still functions today and made the Chengdu Plain China's most productive farmland.

details.label: Background
details.text: The Chengdu Plain was prone to catastrophic flooding from the Min River each spring, then drought the rest of the year. Li Bing, Qin's governor of the Shu region (former state of Shu, modern Sichuan), devised an ingenious solution using the river's own force.

details.label: The Innovation
details.text: Li Bing built a fish-shaped island in the river to divide it into inner (irrigation) and outer (flood control) channels, with a spillway that automatically maintains the correct water level. No dam or lock was needed — the system regulates itself through fluid dynamics.

details.label: Impact
details.text: Dujiangyan has operated for 2,300 years and still irrigates 668,000 hectares — the oldest functioning irrigation system in the world. It made Sichuan China's most productive farmland and fed the population growth that powered Qin's conquests.

events.label: End of the Zhou Dynasty
events.description: The last Zhou king surrendered to Qin, formally ending the longest dynasty in Chinese history. Within 35 years, Qin would unify all of China.

details.label: Background
details.text: By the 3rd century BCE, the Zhou king controlled only the tiny territory around Luoyang — a relic maintained by other states as a symbolic gesture to tradition. When the last king Nan attempted to organize a coalition against Qin in 256 BCE, Qin moved to eliminate even this fiction.

details.label: What Happened
details.text: Qin troops occupied Luoyang. King Nan surrendered and died shortly after. The Zhou royal house — which had nominally ruled China since 1046 BCE — ceased to exist. The last Zhou rump state (Western Zhou) was absorbed into Qin territory.

details.label: Why It Matters
details.text: The Zhou's end was more symbolic than strategic — real power had left the Zhou court centuries earlier. But it marked the formal close of the feudal era and the irreversible momentum toward the unified Qin Empire of 221 BCE, when China became a single state for the first time.
